sentences.text: Well, my name is Sally Marco.
My name-- maiden name is Landowicz.
I was born in Lodz, Poland November 1923, if you want to know exact my date.
November 11.
Well, it was in a large industrial town.
Population was approximately 600,000 people.
And there were quite over 200,000 Jews residing at-- before the war.
And after ghetto was formed, a lot of people were sent out.
And then other people were sent in from different countries like Germany, and Austria, and our adjoining little towns, when they liquidated.
So it kept on being like 200 population almost all the time.
My father's name was Ajzyk Jacov Landovitz.
My mother's name was Esther Landovitz.
And we were eight sisters and a brother.
And my oldest sister was [?
Raica, ?] then was Devora, and Mindel, and Jochewed, and myself, and my brother-- my brother Joseph was older than I was.
And then was my sister Regina, and Ruth, and Lea who did not live through.
And the only ones who survived are Jochewed, myself, Regina, and Ruth.
The rest perished during the war.
My brother died in Feldafing 10 days after the liberation.
And we found his grave.
And we put a matzevah on it.
And then my sister that was older than I, Perl, she died shortly before the war.
And a newspaper was sent to me, and her name-- somebody found the grave.
And-- and her name was on the-- on the grave.
So apparently it was-- must have been very shortly before the end of war that they took the time to put the name on the grave.
Well, we were a middle age-- middle-- middle class family.
My father was a businessman, and he dealt in a business with the government.
It was a government business.
And apparently my father and my uncle were the only ones in Poland to have a-- as Jews, have a business like that.
Most of this business was given to military people that were awarded because of the-- of-- of war achievements.
But it's-- in 1933, when the government in Poland changed, this business was taken away from my father, because he was a Jew.
And my father almost lost his livelihood because there was a large family to support, and there were no means to support.
But somehow my sisters were already older, and some of them went to work.
And we sustained ourselves.
Never ?] [? a ?] [? nice ?] [? man. ?] So it became very difficult, because there was not an income as we were-- my parents were used to.
But somehow we sustained ourselves.
I went to a private school all my life, all my schooled life.
It was a Jewish-- a girls Jewish school, Bais Yaakov, and it was quite expensive.
Not every person in Lodz could afford.
But as I said, my husband-- my father was in business and he could afford, and he emphasized on education.
Although they believed that-- those years, that only boys should have education.
He was intelligent enough to understand that-- that girls as well need education as the same as boys.
Therefore, he sent us to private schools and--
All the children are-- were educated.
I had a sister, the second of the old-- from the oldest, who was one of the most gifted accountants in this city.
She-- she kept a very high position.
And during the war when she escaped the Lodz and went on the Russian side, on the Russian side, the side of Poland occupied by the Russians, and then the German took over.
She did the duties of a bookkeeper for the Germans, even though she was a Jew.
So she was very, very talented, very educated, and so all our children were educated.
When I was growing up we had a lot of friends, non-Jewish friends.
As a matter of fact, the next building to us there was a-- probably more Gentile people than Jewish people.
And I remember that on-- on one occasion I had a girlfriend who was not Jewish.
And I used to do her homework for her.
She was not too bright or was too lazy.
I don't know.
I shouldn't judge her.
And when the war broke out, I-- I was very disappointed, because she was the first one not to know me, to turn her head away when she saw me walking on the street.
And it hurt very much, because I was not 16 years old.
And I really could not understand at that time what prejudice means, because we used to play together.
We also had a little boy that-- in the same building where we lived, that our parents were very close friends.
They were non-Jews.
And mother-- his mother used to come into my mother and take recipes because they liked Jewish food.
And one day that boy was playing with his bow, and his little bow fell through a grate leading to the basement.
And he got very upset that he lost his bow.
And he said to us, when I grow up, I will take a knife and kill all Jews.
Now, he was not provoked that time.
He just lost his bow.
And I thought to myself, he did not hear that at home, because his mother and father are so close with my parents.
Where did the child learn that?
Well, I could not-- I don't think that I-- I had that much experience to know where it really came from.
I still don't know until today where the child picked this up.
So there was a lot-- a lot of anti-Semitism that we-- we were confronted with.
I myself in school days, I was not confronted with anti-Semitism.
Because, as I said, I went to a private school, a religious school, so all the-- all the girls that went there came from approximately the same households as we had.
So I could not part as the time went by.
And when the Germans moved in to occupy Lodz, which was on the 6th of September 1939, we started feeling the pinches.
And the-- the Germans moved in on-- on Friday.
And on Sunday morning at our door was a-- a great knock.
And when we opened the back door, there were two soldiers with pistols in front of them, with a boy that lived across the street from us.
And he showed the soldiers where to get the cigarettes, because my-- my father had a business from the government to sell cigarettes.
So they were looking apparently for cigarettes.
And he-- he brought them in to confiscate everything that was in house.
So from then on, we-- we just started feeling you couldn't walk the streets.
You had to walk on the side-- you could not walk the sidewalks.
And it was very difficult for-- for somebody to go to school and be cut off of school.
Because right away, 1939 September, we could not return to school.
And there was no way of going to a library to get a book to read.
And we had to stay home.
After 5 o'clock, you could not leave your house.
It was a very difficult time to adjust.
We did not realize that as-- the time will come by, it's going to get worse than it was before.
In--
Well, we did not know anything about Nazis.
We knew about the Polish people that-- that belonged to a group that hated Jews.
As a matter of fact, at one time they dispersed after the meeting, and they were running to the streets.
And as I recall today, there was a man locking his shadows.
There were outside shadows of his business and he was shot, because they were running just and killing people.
And three or four people were killed on the way where they were running.
They were just were running wild.
And a man that lived not far away from us was, I recall, he was very tall.
He was driving a motorcycle.
And as he saw that men are running, he went after him and caught him directly on our back door of our-- of our house.
And we could not-- then the police arrived and arrested him.
We could not go out for hours from the room.
First of all, we were very scared.
But even so, we could not leave the house, because that man was caught there.
So anti-Semitism was very, very great.
And if somebody was walking home at night, stones were throwing.
Polish kids used to throw stones at Jewish kids.
And it was-- it was known already, between '33 and '39, we saw a lot of anti-Semitism going on in Lodz.
But you're specifically referring to Polish anti-Semitism?
You're not talking about what was going on in Germany?
No.
Well, we knew about the Crystal Night.
We heard about it.
But you-- you hear about what happened to somebody else, not to you.
We were very much concerned about it.
A lot of people left Poland at that time.
But we always-- I remember my father always said, God should-- God will help.
Not God should help, but God will help.
So you live with the thoughts that God will help.
And the reason was that my father did not want to move.
We were a large family, and it was very difficult to make-- make moves that time.
Well, I think that the Jews were-- I think that the Jews were used to hearing always about inquisitions and pogroms, and all of these things.
And whoever had the luck to-- and then they would say, where are we going to escape to?
There was no Israel or Palestine to escape to, although if you wanted to you, you could-- the gates were closed.
So they had no other choice.
To move from-- from Lodz to another city, they still were not sure that they're going to be safe.
They did not know how much anti-Semitism is [? there. ?] Financially, it was very difficult for people to move on.
Not everybody could afford to just leave everything behind and go.
Yes.
My family was very religious.
My father was very pious Jew.
But he was also a very modern Jew.
In what way?
My-- in-- in a very religious system, a girl could not go out with a boy.
And as I recall, my sisters, my oldest sisters, which were much older than-- was a big distance in age from my oldest to myself, and we knew that they're going out with boys.
And I think deep down in my father's heart, he knew that they're going out with boys.
And I heard once-- overheard it, when my father said once to my sister, if you go out with a boy-- he knew that-- he did not let them know that he knows.
But he says, if you go out with a boy, don't go on dark streets.
Always walk when the streets are lit up.
So my sister would say, why is they are different?
He says, what a boy would allow himself on a dark street, he won't do when the street is lit up.
And you know what?
This remains in my mind, that during the war and after the war, I remembered that.
And I tried not to go with a boy on a dark street.
Just to keep-- not that I was holy or anything, but it was in my mind, all the things that I remembered from-- from home.
I think helped a decency to make-- to make a person out of myself, the way I really wanted to be.
And it helped a lot.
If it comes to-- to thinking the same way?
Yes.
Oh, yes.
Very much so.
They always discuss things.
They never-- there was never anything done in our household without talking over.
The older sisters, my sisters, used to explain to me things that, you know, mothers did not explain things to-- to younger children.
But my sisters did explain to me.
As a matter of fact, my sisters took me one to a-- once to a dance, was a-- a WIZO, the women's Zionist organization.
And they made a dance.
And they took me along and dressed me up I-- in a navy blue velvet outfit-- suit.
And when I came to the door, the-- the man that were taking-- getting the tickets says, look at those children they're bringing now to a dance.
[LAUGHS] So I-- they try to make me grown up faster than I could to do that.
And I have very-- very-- very good memories for my, you know, living with my family.
Unfortunately, it was destroyed in 1939.
I could not take it any longer.
My father used to-- and we could not go out on the street.
My father used to sit with me in the kitchen and play cards.
I remember that very vividly--
That my father was so religious, gave me the first cigarettes to smoke.
And he said to my mother, one day, she's going to go to through that ceiling with her head.
Because he saw that-- how impatient I was to the situation, that my education was cut off, that I really tried to have.
And I could not go to visit girlfriend, I had to be home at 5 o'clock when the-- there was no television, or telephone, or anything like that, so you really were confined to the house.
And at the end of November of 1939, I escaped.
I left home.
Almost without consent of my parents.
My mother cried.
And my father said that, you won't be able to do a thing with her.
She decided to go.
She's going to go.
And we went.
I jumped a train.
No, with another-- with another couple.
A young friend of--
I wanted to get away from the Germans.
I just could not cope with the situation, with the way of living.
I did not think that I will be able to-- to adjust to a living like that.
And I also thought that if I go and see how the situation is, like an early bird goes to find a worm, I thought that if I go and I find a way to get my family out.
And that's what I did.
I was-- I was not-- yeah, I was 16 when I left.
It was-- it was in end of November.
So I just turned 16.
And when I came to Brest-Litovsk, there were a lot, a lot of people homeless that came.
And somehow we found an older woman with a daughter who had one room, lived in one room.
And she rented to us-- to me a cot.
And I noticed the sign when we went to register, I noticed the sign it says, waitress wanted.
So everybody was-- the whole group that was working, there were laughing.
This sign must be from before the war, but I insisted.
I want to go.
So the evening when we went, I went back.
I found out it was a Turkish konditorei.
It was like for Turks that came to Poland and they opened bakeries and like a coffee shop.
And when I talk to them, the man started-- you know, I was so young and my hands, he saw that I-- I never did dishes.
So he tried to discourage me.
And I persisted that I want to work.
And I got the job.
I worked there till the end of December.
And I-- I-- I fell from two steps.
And I broke a vessel in my hip.
So I was laying.
Then I went back and I decided that I'm not happy by myself.
I was laying at night and crying what happened to-- I was not sure what happened to my family.
I could not communicate.
So I decided to go back.
And on the way back I found people, good and bad.
And it was the greatest experience of my life that I have, very unpleasant.
I was caught [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the border going back, and I was released.
And then I found out that I cannot go back on the train.
I had to go with wagons.
I have no money.
Because on the border they took everything away from me.
Well, first the Russians, and then when we came to the German border, the German searched us and they took the rest whatever we had.
This was January of 1940.
When I came to a small town, there was-- were Jewish families.
And I went in and they told me that you cannot go to train.
But I decided to go to-- to the train station and find out if I can get a ticket.
And I asked for the head of the whole line that was from-- the little town was near [?
Moica ?].
And the whole line went to Warsaw, the-- the train line.
And I walked over to the cashier and I says, I would like to see the Gauleiter, who was the head of the-- and she showed me, and it was an older man with-- with a hat, [INAUDIBLE].
There was a dead head on his cap.
And I told him a story that I went to relatives in Poland.
And the war came, which we did have relatives in Bialystok.
And the war came, and I could not return, because there was a border.
And I would like to go back.
Because I have my parents.
And he said to me, where do you want to go?
And I said, Lodz.
He says, oh no, there you cannot go because Lodz belongs already to the-- to the Reich, to the German that they called the-- the Third Reich.
But I said, well, I have relatives in Warsaw.
If I cannot go to Lodz, I will go to Warsaw.
He says, well to Warsaw you can go.
Why don't you go?
He says, because I cannot buy a ticket on the train.
And he says, why can't you do that?
And I says, because I'm Jewish.
And he looked at me and he says, well, who told you that you are Jewish?
He says, when you look at my nose you can tell that I'm Jewish.
And he kept on saying, now, who told you that you were Jewish?
And I kept repeating the same thing three, four times.
And he said, you know what?
If they say that the Jews are very smart, but if one is dumb, it's the dumbest.
And he told me later, he said, you come 5 o'clock.
And you have the exact amount of money.
And through the cashier's hat that man handed me a ticket.
And when it came time to go out, and I was staying-- and believe me I was shaking like a leaf.
It was 40 below zero.
And some Polacks were staying in back of me, and I did not move.
This you can be sure.
And one Polack said to-- to the other, look at that Jew, she's pushing herself.
Now, when the gate opened, it was not the regular station.
It was just a-- a-- a stop.
It was a small gate.
As the gate opened, I flew out.
So somebody gave me a push.
Apparently that-- that Gauleiter figured that if I'm caught, I'm going to tell that he gave me the ticket.
I was the first one on the train.
When I came to [?
Shedlets, ?] which was a stop.
We had to change trains.
And I was passing to show my ticket.
A Polack caught me, and he says, this is a Jew, and called over the Gestapo, and turned me over to the Gestapo.
And there were two soldiers with bayonets, with rifles and bayonets.
And I'm staying right in the middle.
And as they turned their heads, I escaped.
I did not realize what I'm doing.
Because with their bayonets-- And I was hiding underneath, I ran.
It was a huge station.
I ran when I saw a-- a bench, and people were sitting on the bench.
I ran there and hid underneath the bench.
I could not be seen because people were sitting there.
And that Polack came and-- and fished me out from there.
And he did not turn me over to the Gestapo on the station, but he took me already upstairs to the station.
And I pleaded, and I moaned and yoaned, and they took me to-- to a jail.
I sat there for maybe three or four hours.
They took me to jail, and as I came in there, there was a jailer, also German.
And he said to me, you know, you committed a crime.
You have to be in jail.
And I said, I did not commit a crime.
And he says, you have to pay a fee for crossing, whatever.
But because you took a train, and you're not told-- it's a penalty that you have to pay.
I had no-- nothing.
I had like 20 zloty which is-- but I had-- I remember I had a [? cloot. ?] I don't know, for silver.
But it was like a silver dollar.
And I said to him, I want to give you this.
And you take this money.
What this was worth, a 10 zloty or whatever.
Do you have a paper of 10 zloty?
And he said, yes.
I says, could you exchange it and keep this as a talisman?
And wear-- you put it near you heart.
And if you send any place this will save you from being killed.
Now this man turned around and cried.
And took out a tissue, and put the-- the money in and put it straight on the left side into his pocket.
And took us out.
There was another woman that was from Warsaw.
And she was-- they brought her in later.
And they took us both out.
And they took us to a children's orphanage to sleep over the night.
And they were told to give me a bed, because I'm sick.
But the woman in charge never gave me the bed.
So we [? sat ?] there the whole day.
And the following day I took a wagon-- horse and buggy and went to another city.
And this way from one city to the other.
I came to Warsaw.
My older sister lived that time in Warsaw with her husband and child.
And I got very sick.
I had pneumonia.
And I met there a woman that her children were my friends.
And she introduced me to a restaurant owner, and I got a job there.
And people were very wonderful.
Really they-- a brother of hers came and-- I don't know if you know what bankis are?
Mm-hmm.
You know what those-- and he came--
Glass, yeah.
And he-- he put this on, and they wouldn't let me come the next day to work.
They're little-- they're very little and like a glass.
And they put alcohol in-- with a light in it.
And they put on the back.
And this apparently takes out infection or whatever that is.
And the next day I was well.
So from-- my parents found out the time in Warsaw and I cannot continue.
And I made up my mind not to go back to the Lodz again.
I wanted my parents and my sisters to come to Warsaw and go back to Brest-Litovsk where I came from that time.
But my mother came to Warsaw.
And she forced me to come back, and that's when I came back to the-- back into the ghetto.
